focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

The Bebop had been in free-drift for six hours and already sounded poorer.

With the main engines cold, the ship let every small noise travel. The hull ticked as it gave up heat. The life-support fans pushed a tired hum through the vents. Somewhere behind Jet's left shoulder, one loose spoon in the galley drawer kept tapping metal on metal every time the ship corrected its drift by a hair. Mars turned under them in a slow red sweep through the forward ports, the Hellas basin all rust scars and dark cuts, beautiful in the same useless way an expensive watch was beautiful when you couldn't afford lunch.

Jet stood in the galley with the cold-storage door open and took inventory like he was conducting an autopsy.

He had three bell peppers, and one of them was already wrinkling at the stem. Half an onion was wrapped in wax paper. There was a bottle of soy sauce with enough left to wet a pan and not much more, plus a packet of textured protein flakes that had gone stale so long ago they had become a geological feature. There was no beef.

The orange traffic summons lay under the salt tin so it wouldn't curl in the damp. `RECKLESS FLYING IN SETTLEMENT AIRSPACE`, it said in the neat bureaucratic block print of people who never had to choose between fuel and dinner. Eight thousand woolongs. Jet had turned it face down twice. It kept making its way back up like something alive.

He shut the cold-storage door with his hip, set the peppers on the board, and started cutting.

Across the common room, Spike was on the couch with one ankle over the other knee and a receiver balanced on his stomach. He kept rolling the dial with two fingers, slow, patient, like he was trying to tune in a station from another decade. What came out was mostly hiss with the occasional chop o...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...y bleeding across the locker floor.

"Yeah," Spike said. "I'll tell you."

It came out close enough to truth to pass if you weren't looking directly at it.

The dockmaster on the local channel started yelling about departure sequence. Jet cut him off with a code string and a promise he did not mean about filing clean. The Bebop rose slow and heavy from the paid berth, casting a wide shadow over the concrete lot and the crooked service mast and the man below still carrying his crate. Dust rolled outward in red sheets. Laundry snapped itself horizontal on the line between the poles.

Tharsis opened under them in layers of roof metal, transit lanes, rust streaks, greenhouse glass gone cloudy at the edges. For a second Spike saw the alley behind Mama Yun's, the empty slot, and steam leaking from the noodle shop vent into the cold. Then the ship banked and the settlement fell away behind them.

South, the land roughened and widened. The map on the bridge screens redrew itself around basin contours and old roads half lost to dust. The blinking point ahead kept moving with patient confidence, not fleeing, not waiting either, and making them come.

Jet set the course.

Faye strapped in at last, muttering to herself.

Ed hummed over the nav board and fed route updates to the system in little bursts of delight.

Ein braced his paws and sneezed when the cabin pressure shifted.

Spike stood behind them with one hand on the seatback and the folded manifest against his chest and watched the southbound signal burn clean on the screen. There were four people on the Bebop, one ship ahead of them, and four different reasons to follow.

From up here Mars looked empty enough to hide anybody.

That was the problem. It looked that way right before it found room for the past to climb back in.
